Various kinds of sedatives can be distinguished, but the majority of them affect the [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") [gamma-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/Gamma-Aminobutyric_acid "Gamma-Aminobutyric acid") (GABA). Most sedatives produce relaxing effects by increasing GABA activity.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

A **sedative** or **tranquilizer**[[note 1]](#cite_note-1) is a substance that induces [sedation](/wiki/Sedation "Sedation") by reducing [irritability](/wiki/Irritability "Irritability")[[1]](#cite_note-urlJohns_Hopkins_Colon_Cancer_Center_-_Glossary_S-2) or [excitement](/wiki/Psychomotor_agitation "Psychomotor agitation").[[2]](#cite_note-3) They are [central nervous system](/wiki/Central_nervous_system "Central nervous system") (CNS) [depressants](/wiki/Depressant "Depressant") and interact with brain activity, causing its deceleration. Various kinds of sedatives can be distinguished, but the majority of them affect the [neurotransmitter](/wiki/Neurotransmitter "Neurotransmitter") [gamma-aminobutyric acid](/wiki/Gamma-Aminobutyric_acid "Gamma-Aminobutyric acid") (GABA). Most sedatives produce relaxing effects by increasing GABA activity.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

This group is related to [hypnotics](/wiki/Hypnotic "Hypnotic"). The term *sedative* describes drugs that serve to calm or [relieve anxiety](/wiki/Anxiolytic "Anxiolytic"), whereas the term *hypnotic* describes drugs whose main purpose is to initiate, sustain, or lengthen sleep. Because these two functions frequently overlap, and because drugs in this class generally produce dose-dependent effects (ranging from [anxiolysis](/wiki/Anxiolysis "Anxiolysis") to loss of consciousness), they are often referred to collectively as *sedative–hypnotic* drugs.[[4]](#cite_note-Pharmacologic_Basis_of_Therapeutics-5)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

There is some overlap between the terms "sedative" and "hypnotic".

Advances in pharmacology have permitted more specific targeting of receptors, and greater selectivity of agents, which necessitates greater precision when describing these agents and their effects:

* "[Anxiolytic](/wiki/Anxiolytic "Anxiolytic")" refers specifically to the effect upon [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety "Anxiety"). (However, some [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepines "Benzodiazepines") can be all three: sedatives, hypnotics, and anxiolytics).
* "Tranquiliser" can refer to anxiolytics or [antipsychotics](/wiki/Antipsychotic "Antipsychotic").

### "Chemical cosh"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: \"Chemical cosh\"")]

The term "chemical [cosh](/wiki/Club_(weapon) "Club (weapon)")" (*cosh* being a term for a blunt weapon such as a club) is sometimes used colloquially for a strong sedative, particularly for:

* Widespread dispensation of [antipsychotic drugs](/wiki/Antipsychotic "Antipsychotic") in residential care to make people with [dementia](/wiki/Dementia "Dementia") easier to manage.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

## Therapeutic use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Therapeutic use")]

[Doctors](/wiki/Physician "Physician") and [veterinarians](/wiki/Veterinarians "Veterinarians") often administer sedatives to patients in order to dull the patient's anxiety related to painful or anxiety-provoking procedures. Although sedatives do not relieve pain, they can be a useful adjunct to [analgesics](/wiki/Analgesic "Analgesic") in preparing patients for [surgery](/wiki/Surgery "Surgery"), and are commonly given to patients before they are [anaesthetized](/wiki/Anaesthesia "Anaesthesia"), or before other highly uncomfortable and invasive procedures like [cardiac catheterization](/wiki/Cardiac_catheterization "Cardiac catheterization"), [endoscopy](/wiki/Endoscopy "Endoscopy"), and [colonoscopy](/wiki/Colonoscopy "Colonoscopy").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Risks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Risks")]

### Sedative dependence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Sedative dependence")]

Some sedatives can cause psychological and physical dependence when taken regularly over a period of time, even at therapeutic doses.[[6]](#cite_note-7)[[7]](#cite_note-8)[[8]](#cite_note-9)[[9]](#cite_note-10) Dependent users may get withdrawal symptoms ranging from restlessness and insomnia to convulsions and death. When users become psychologically dependent, they feel as if they need the drug to function, although physical dependence does not necessarily occur, particularly with a short course of use. In both types of dependencies, finding and using the sedative becomes the focus in life. Both physical and psychological dependence can be treated with therapy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Misuse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Misuse")]

Main article: [Drug overdose](/wiki/Drug_overdose "Drug overdose")

Further information: [Combined drug intoxication](/wiki/Combined_drug_intoxication "Combined drug intoxication")

See also: [Benzodiazepine overdose](/wiki/Benzodiazepine_overdose "Benzodiazepine overdose") and [Barbiturate overdose](/wiki/Barbiturate_overdose "Barbiturate overdose")

Many sedatives can be misused, but barbiturates and benzodiazepines are responsible for most of the problems with sedative use due to their widespread recreational or non-medical use. People who have difficulty dealing with stress, anxiety or sleeplessness may overuse or become dependent on sedatives. Some [heroin](/wiki/Heroin "Heroin") users may take them either to supplement their drug or to substitute for it. [Stimulant](/wiki/Stimulant "Stimulant") users may take sedatives to calm excessive jitteriness. Others take sedatives recreationally to relax and forget their worries. [Barbiturate overdose](/wiki/Barbiturate_overdose "Barbiturate overdose") is a factor in nearly one-third of all reported drug-related deaths. These include [suicides](/wiki/Suicide "Suicide") and accidental drug poisonings. Benzodiazepines comparatively have a wider margin of safety and rarely result in overdose unless mixed with other CNS depressants.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Accidental deaths sometimes occur when a [drowsy](/wiki/Somnolence "Somnolence"), [confused](/wiki/Confusion "Confusion") user repeats doses, or when sedatives are taken with [alcohol](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverages "Alcoholic beverages").

A study from the United States found that in 2011, sedatives and hypnotics were a leading source of adverse drug events (ADEs) seen in the hospital setting: Approximately 2.8% of all ADEs present on admission and 4.4% of ADEs that originated during a hospital stay were caused by a sedative or hypnotic drug.[[11]](#cite_note-12) A second study noted that a total of 70,982 sedative exposures were reported to U.S. [poison](/wiki/Poison "Poison") control centers in 1998, of which 2310 (3.2%) resulted in major [toxicity](/wiki/Toxicity "Toxicity") and 89 (0.1%) resulted in death. About half of all the people admitted to emergency rooms in the U.S. as a result of nonmedical use of sedatives have a legitimate prescription for the drug, but have taken an excessive dose or combined it with alcohol or other drugs.[[12]](#cite_note-13)

There are also serious [paradoxical reactions](/wiki/Paradoxical_reactions "Paradoxical reactions") that may occur in conjunction with the use of sedatives that lead to unexpected results in some individuals. Malcolm Lader at the Institute of Psychiatry in London estimates the incidence of these adverse reactions at about 5%, even in short-term use of the drugs. The paradoxical reactions may consist of [depression](/wiki/Depression_(mood) "Depression (mood)"), with or without [suicidal tendencies](/wiki/Suicidal_ideation "Suicidal ideation"), [phobias](/wiki/Phobias "Phobias"), aggressiveness, [violent](/wiki/Violent "Violent") behavior and symptoms sometimes misdiagnosed as [psychosis](/wiki/Psychosis "Psychosis").[[13]](#cite_note-14)

### Dangers of combining sedatives and alcohol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Dangers of combining sedatives and alcohol")]

Further information: [Combined Drug Intoxication](/wiki/Combined_Drug_Intoxication "Combined Drug Intoxication")

Sedatives and alcohol are sometimes combined [recreationally](/wiki/Recreational_drug_use "Recreational drug use") or carelessly. Since alcohol is a strong depressant that slows [brain](/wiki/Brain "Brain") function and [depresses respiration](/wiki/Hypoventilation "Hypoventilation"), the two substances compound each other's actions and this combination can prove fatal.

### Worsening of psychiatric symptoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Worsening of psychiatric symptoms")]

The long-term use of benzodiazepines may have a similar effect on the brain as [alcohol](/wiki/Alcoholic_beverage "Alcoholic beverage"), and are also implicated in [depression](/wiki/Mood_disorder#Substance-induced "Mood disorder"), [anxiety](/wiki/Anxiety#Substance-induced "Anxiety"), [post-traumatic stress disorder](/wiki/Post-traumatic_stress_disorder "Post-traumatic stress disorder") (PTSD), [mania](/wiki/Mania "Mania"), [psychosis](/wiki/Psychosis "Psychosis"), [sleep disorders](/wiki/Insomnia#Substance-induced "Insomnia"), [sexual dysfunction](/wiki/Sexual_dysfunction "Sexual dysfunction"), [delirium](/wiki/Delirium "Delirium"), and [neurocognitive disorders](/wiki/Cognitive_disorder "Cognitive disorder") (including benzodiazepine-induced persisting dementia which persists even after the medications are stopped).[[14]](#cite_note-:12-15) As with alcohol, the effects of [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepine "Benzodiazepine") on neurochemistry, such as decreased levels of [serotonin](/wiki/Serotonin "Serotonin") and [norepinephrine](/wiki/Norepinephrine "Norepinephrine"), are believed to be responsible for their effects on mood and anxiety.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-ashman-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)[[20]](#cite_note-21) Additionally, benzodiazepines can indirectly cause or worsen other psychiatric symptoms (e.g., mood, anxiety, psychosis, irritability) by worsening sleep (i.e., benzodiazepine-induced sleep disorder). Benzodiazepines are commonly used to treat insomnia in the short-term (both prescribed and self-medicated), but worsen sleep in the long-term. While benzodiazepines can put people to sleep, they disrupt [sleep architecture](/wiki/Sleep_architecture "Sleep architecture"): decreasing sleep time, delaying time to [REM sleep](/wiki/REM_sleep "REM sleep"), and decreasing deep [slow-wave sleep](/wiki/Slow-wave_sleep "Slow-wave sleep") (the most restorative part of sleep for both energy and mood).[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-24)

### Dementia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Dementia")]

Sedatives and hypnotics should be avoided in people with dementia,[[24]](#cite_note-25) according to the [medication appropriateness tool for co‐morbid health conditions in dementia criteria](/wiki/Medication_appropriateness_tool_for_co%E2%80%90morbid_health_conditions_in_dementia_criteria "Medication appropriateness tool for co‐morbid health conditions in dementia criteria").[[25]](#cite_note-26) The use of these medications can further impede cognitive function for people with dementia, who are also more sensitive to side effects of medications.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Amnesia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Amnesia")]

Sedatives can sometimes leave the patient with long-term or short-term [amnesia](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia").
[Lorazepam](/wiki/Lorazepam "Lorazepam") is one such pharmacological agent that can cause [anterograde amnesia](/wiki/Anterograde_amnesia "Anterograde amnesia"). [Intensive care unit](/wiki/Intensive_care_unit "Intensive care unit") patients who receive higher doses over longer periods, typically via [IV drip](/wiki/Intravenous_therapy "Intravenous therapy"), are more likely to experience such side effects.
Additionally, the prolonged use of tranquilizers increases the risk of obsessive and compulsive disorder, where the person becomes unaware whether he has performed a scheduled activity or not, he may also repetitively perform tasks and still re-performs the same task trying to make-up for continuous doubts. Remembering names that were earlier known becomes an issue such that the memory loss becomes apparent.

### Disinhibition and crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sedative&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Disinhibition and crime")]

Sedatives — most commonly [alcohol](/wiki/Ethylalcohol "Ethylalcohol"),[[26]](#cite_note-27) but also [GHB](/wiki/Gamma-hydroxybutyrate "Gamma-hydroxybutyrate"), [flunitrazepam](/wiki/Flunitrazepam "Flunitrazepam") (Rohypnol), and to a lesser extent, [temazepam](/wiki/Temazepam "Temazepam") (Restoril) and [midazolam](/wiki/Midazolam "Midazolam") (Versed)[[27]](#cite_note-28) — have been reported for their use as [date rape](/wiki/Date_rape "Date rape") drugs (also called a [Mickey Finn](/wiki/Mickey_Finn_(drugs) "Mickey Finn (drugs)")) and being administered to unsuspecting patrons in bars or guests at parties to reduce the intended victims' defenses.

Statistical overviews suggest that the use of sedative-spiked drinks for robbing people is actually much more common than their use for rape.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Cases of criminals taking Rohypnol themselves before they commit crimes have also been reported, as the loss of inhibitions from the drug may increase their confidence to commit the offense, and the [amnesia](/wiki/Amnesia "Amnesia") produced by the drug makes it difficult for police to interrogate them if they are caught.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
